content: APJ Abdul Kalam was born on October 15, 1931, in Rameswaram, Tamil Nadu, to a boat owner. The family was not wealthy. Young Kalam delivered newspapers before school to help his family. His teacher Siva Subramania Iyer ignited his curiosity and told him: 'You must dream and then work — and dreams will become reality.'

Kalam led the development of India's first indigenous satellite launch vehicle (SLV-III) which successfully deployed the Rohini satellite in 1980. He later directed India's ballistic missile program, developing the Agni and Prithvi missiles. This earned him the title 'Missile Man of India' and made India a significant space and defense power.

Kalam joined ISRO (Indian Space Research Organisation) in 1969 and became project director of the SLV-III program. Under his leadership, India developed indigenous capability to launch satellites. He believed space technology should serve common people — from weather prediction for farmers to telemedicine for rural areas.

Kalam served as the 11th President of India from 2002 to 2007, becoming known as the 'People's President'. Unlike many politicians, he was a scientist who maintained his connection with students and ordinary citizens. He made the Rashtrapati Bhavan accessible to schoolchildren and continued teaching even during his presidency.

Kalam authored 'India 2020: A Vision for the New Millennium', outlining a plan to transform India into a developed nation by 2020. The vision focused on five key areas: agriculture, infrastructure, education, healthcare, and technology. He believed India's greatest resource was its young population.

Kalam's most famous quote is: 'Dream is not what you see in sleep. Dream is something that does not let you sleep.' He believed in four principles: have a great aim, acquire knowledge, work hard, and persevere. He failed his first air force pilot test but did not give up, redirecting his passion to rocket science.

Kalam played a key role in India's Pokhran-II nuclear tests in May 1998 as chief scientific adviser. The tests demonstrated India's nuclear capability and established India as a nuclear-armed state. Kalam advocated for nuclear energy for peaceful purposes while maintaining a minimum credible deterrent.

Kalam's autobiography 'Wings of Fire' traces his journey from a small-town newspaper boy to one of India's most celebrated scientists. The book emphasizes that great achievement comes from ordinary beginnings when combined with hard work, curiosity, and mentorship. It has inspired millions of students across India and the world.

Kalam died on July 27, 2015, while delivering a lecture to students at IIM Shillong — doing what he loved most: teaching young people. His death at a podium in front of students was seen as fitting for a man who dedicated his life to inspiring youth. India declared a seven-day state mourning period.
